task_name: One-Shot Write Permit design (design-only, no build)
goal: Capture the One-Shot Write Permit architecture as a design-only document: replace the three standing write env-flags with per-act, single-use, time-limited permits that separate capability (the machine can write), permission (this one write is approved), and policy (who may issue). Record Codex's two hard requirements (content-hash binding and atomic single-use), four further same-risk-class decisions, the three autonomy levels, the kill-switch role, and the two-service build that BP-144 will carry out. No code, no register, no corridor change, no flag runtime change.
scope_out: Do not write any code, register, route, schema, or test.
Do not change the runtime behavior of the three write flags.
Do not modify the Maya corridor, opusSmartPush, opusPatchMode, or any builder module.
Do not update the anchor docs (CLAUDE-CONTEXT, SESSION-LOG, STATE); their refresh is a separate noted thread.
Do not start the BP-144 build in this task.
claims: docs/BP-143-ONESHOT-WRITE-PERMIT-DESIGN.md exists and is design-only with an explicit no-build banner naming BP-144 as the build contract.

The document separates capability, permission, and policy, and defines the permit data model (permitId, issuer, target, op, expectedBaseSha, contentHash, expiry, usedAt, status, policySnapshot, auditRef).
The document records Codex's two hard requirements in intent: content-hash binding to the concrete write, and server-side atomic single-use (compare-and-set).

The document adds four further same-risk-class decisions: hash recomputed at the builder byte-exit, create-vs-update plus base-sha pin, a policy-independent protected-path floor, and one-attempt retry semantics.

The document states BP-144 is a two-service build: maya-core register plus atomic consume, and bluepilot threading permit fields through corridor and SmartPush, noting assessCorridor does not yet know these fields.
The document keeps MAYA_BUILDER_REPO_WRITE_ENABLED as a fail-closed kill-switch and marks the two builder flags for replacement by permits.
The permit mechanism is described as a genuine new build, not a small docking onto the existing approval_ticket and sha256 helper.
assumptions: Phase B is live as of 2026-06-01; the first real sandbox write succeeded (commit f95fa11).
Local main is at 019e7bf; STATE.md and SESSION-LOG.md are current to BP-142.
builderApprovalArtifacts already provides approvalId, an approval_ticket, and a sha256 helper, but no permit register, no atomic consume, no expiry, and no write-binding exist yet.
The permit register will live in maya-core (aicos-registry/master); the GitHub PAT and the actual write live in bluepilot-builder.
dod: docs/BP-143-ONESHOT-WRITE-PERMIT-DESIGN.md exists under docs/ and contains a no-build banner naming BP-144.

The document contains the permit data model, the state machine (issued -> consumed atomically -> done/failed, one permit = one attempt), the two hard requirements, the four further decisions, the three autonomy levels, and the kill-switch role.
The document contains the two-service BP-144 section naming assessCorridor as not yet knowing the permit fields.
contracts/BP-143.json validates as task_type doc_task with no code or tool files in allowed_files.
review-packets/BP-143.md records the design scope, the corrections folded in, and verification evidence.
stop_conditions: Writing any code, register, route, schema, or test becomes necessary.
Changing the corridor, SmartPush, or any builder module becomes necessary.
Changing the runtime behavior of any write flag becomes necessary.
A new architecture decision outside the permit design becomes necessary.
prior_task_findings: BP-142 cleared the final adapter blocker and Phase B went live: idea -> builder phases -> four gates -> Maya corridor -> verified sandbox commit f95fa11. The three standing write flags are unsafe as a permanent state. BP-143 designs the One-Shot Write Permit that replaces them; the build is deferred to BP-144 as a two-service change. Codex confirmed the design document is good enough and supplied two hard requirements plus the assessCorridor and STATE.md branch findings.
